cards.title: The passive (present simple, past simple, will)
cards.subtitle: The passive (present simple, past simple, will)

sections.title: Use
items: When the person responsible for the action is unknown: My laptop **was stolen** last week.
When the person responsible for the action is unimportant or obvious: **Was** the homework **checked** today?

sections.title: Useful notes
items: Look at the active sentence. The verb is *broke* and the object is *the classroom window*.
The object of the active sentence (*the classroom window*) becomes the subject of the passive sentence. *The classroom window* …
Then we need the verb *be* in the same tense as the verb in the active sentence. Here, *broke* is past simple, so we need *was*. *The classroom window was* …
Then we need the past participle of the verb in the active sentence. The past participle of *break* is *broken*. *The classroom window was broken* …
Finally, we finish the sentence in the right way. ***The classroom window was broken yesterday.***

sections.title: Be careful
items: When the verb in a passive sentence is a phrasal verb, don't forget to include the particle. They **turned off** the lights. → The lights were **turned off**.
Some verbs have irregular past participle forms (e.g. break → broken).

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with the correct passive form
exercises.title: A: Complete with the correct passive form
exercises.instructions: Use the verbs in brackets in the present simple passive, past simple passive or will passive.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the verbs in brackets in the present simple passive, past simple passive or will passive.

items.sentence: The school uniforms ...... (wash) every week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is habitual action + no agent, so the correct answer is "are washed".

wrongOptions.wash: The bare infinitive (wash) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The concert hall ...... (decorate) for the festival last month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is finished past time, so the correct answer is "was decorated".

wrongOptions.decorate: The bare infinitive (decorate) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

items.sentence: The exam results ...... (email) tomorrow morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future time, so the correct answer is "will be emailed".

wrongOptions.email: The bare infinitive (email) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

items.sentence: These phones ...... (produce) in South Korea.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is general truth about where things are made, so the correct answer is "are produced".

wrongOptions.produce: The bare infinitive (produce) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (produce) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: The reports ...... (not finish) yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is yesterday, so the correct answer is "weren't finished".

wrongOptions.not finish: The bare infinitive (not finish) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: A finished past-time expression usually requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time — watch irregular past forms.

items.sentence: ...... the prizes ...... (give) after the competition?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is passive question, so the correct answer is "Will, be given".

wrongOptions.give: The bare infinitive (give) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Active voice puts the wrong focus here — passive needs be + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses be + past participle — the focus is on the action, not the doer.

items.sentence: All essays ...... (mark) by our English teacher.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is the teacher marks them, so the correct answer is "are marked".

wrongOptions.mark: The bare infinitive (mark) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (mark) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: My keys ...... (not discover) anywhere after the trip.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past negative + nowhere found, so the correct answer is "weren't discovered".

wrongOptions.not discover: The bare infinitive (not discover) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (not discover) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

exercises.title: B: Change active to passive
exercises.instructions: Rewrite each sentence in the passive.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite each sentence in the passive.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Active, so the sentence is "Coffee is grown in Brazil.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: People grow coffee in Brazil. → Coffee is grown in Brazil.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Active: Check how "Active" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: Someone damaged the school gate last night.

cues: Someone damaged the school gate last night.

items.answer: The school gate was damaged last night.
acceptedAnswers: The school gate was damaged last night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Past passive, so the sentence is "The school gate was damaged last night.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: Someone damaged the school gate last night. → The school gate was damaged last night.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Past: Check how "Past" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.passive: Check how "passive" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: They will announce the winners next week.

cues: They will announce the winners next week.

items.answer: The winners will be announced next week.
acceptedAnswers: The winners will be announced next week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Future passive, so the sentence is "The winners will be announced next week.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: They will announce the winners next week. → The winners will be announced next week.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Future: Check how "Future" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.passive: Check how "passive" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: They don't allow phones in class.

cues: They don't allow phones in class.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Allow, so the sentence is "Phones aren't allowed in class.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: They don't allow phones in class. → Phones aren't allowed in class.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Allow: Check how "Allow" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: Did they cancel the football match?

cues: Did they cancel the football match?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Did they cancel?, so the sentence is "Was the football match cancelled?".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: Did they cancel the football match? → Was the football match cancelled?
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.Did: Check how "Did" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.they: Check how "they" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: The chef prepared the food this morning.

cues: The chef prepared the food this morning.

items.answer: The food was prepared this morning.
acceptedAnswers: The food was prepared this morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The chef prepared, so the sentence is "The food was prepared this morning.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: The chef prepared the food this morning. → The food was prepared this morning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice — be + past participle when the action matters more than who did it.

wrongOptions.The: Check how "The" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.chef: Check how "chef" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

exercises.title: C: Circle the correct form
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: by thousands of tourists each year.
items.originalSentence: This castle **visited / is visited / was visiting** by thousands of tourists each year.
items.completedSentence: This castle is visited by thousands of tourists each year.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "is visited" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "is visited" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "visited" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.visited: "visited" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("by thousands of tourists each year.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.visited: "visited" doesn't fit here → "is visited" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("by thousands of tourists each year.").
optionContrast.was visiting: "was visiting" doesn't fit here → "is visited" does, because Only "is visited" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The project **completed / was completed / is completing** yesterday afternoon.
items.completedSentence: The project was completed yesterday afternoon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "was completed" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "was completed" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "completed" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.completed: "completed" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("yesterday afternoon.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.completed: "completed" doesn't fit here → "was completed" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("yesterday afternoon.").
optionContrast.is completing: "is completing" doesn't fit here → "was completed" does, because Only "was completed" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The new shopping centre **will open / will be opened / is opened** next month.
items.completedSentence: The new shopping centre will be opened next month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "will be opened" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "will be opened" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "will open" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.will open: "will open" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("next month.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.will open: "will open" doesn't fit here → "will be opened" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("next month.").
optionContrast.is opened: "is opened" doesn't fit here → "will be opened" does, because Only "will be opened" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The floors **are cleaned / clean / were cleaning** every night.
items.completedSentence: The floors are cleaned every night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "are cleaned" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "are cleaned" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "clean" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.clean: "clean" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("every night.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.clean: "clean" doesn't fit here → "are cleaned" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("every night.").
optionContrast.were cleaning: "were cleaning" doesn't fit here → "are cleaned" does, because Only "are cleaned" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The windows **wasn't open / wasn't opened / didn't opened** during the storm.
items.completedSentence: The windows wasn't opened during the storm.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "wasn't opened" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "wasn't opened" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "wasn't open" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.wasn't open: "wasn't open" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("during the storm.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.wasn't open: "wasn't open" doesn't fit here → "wasn't opened" does, because "wasn't open" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("during the storm.").
optionContrast.didn't opened: "didn't opened" doesn't fit here → "wasn't opened" does, because Only "wasn't opened" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.originalSentence: **Is the parcel delivered / Is the parcel delivery / Does the parcel delivered** every morning?
items.completedSentence: Is the parcel delivered every morning?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "Is the parcel delivered" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "Is the parcel delivered" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Is the parcel delivery" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.Is the parcel delivery: "Is the parcel delivery" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("every morning?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.Is the parcel delivery: "Is the parcel delivery" doesn't fit here → "Is the parcel delivered" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("every morning?").
optionContrast.Does the parcel delivered: "Does the parcel delivered" doesn't fit here → "Is the parcel delivered" does, because Only "Is the parcel delivered" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: D: Complete with by or with
exercises.title: D: Complete with by or with
exercises.instructions: Write by or with in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write by or with in each gap.

items.sentence: The story was written ...... a famous author.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is the agent who wrote, so the correct answer is "by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The vegetables were cut ...... a sharp knife.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is the tool used, so the correct answer is "with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The school play was organised ...... the drama club.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is who organised, so the correct answer is "by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: The floor was cleaned ...... a mop.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is instrument used, so the correct answer is "with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: The mural was painted ...... local artists.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is who painted, so the correct answer is "by".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: The photo was taken ...... a digital camera.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is device used, so the correct answer is "with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: E: Phrasal verbs in the passive
exercises.title: E: Phrasal verbs in the passive
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct passive form of the phrasal verb in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct passive form of the phrasal verb in brackets.

items.sentence: The tables ...... (put away) after the event.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is after the event, so the correct answer is "were put away".

wrongOptions.put away: The bare infinitive (put away) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Active voice puts the wrong focus here — passive needs be + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses be + past participle — the focus is on the action, not the doer.

items.sentence: All the posters ...... (put up) before the concert started.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is before the concert, so the correct answer is "were put up".

wrongOptions.put up: The bare infinitive (put up) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (put up) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: The computers ...... (switch off) every evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is every evening, so the correct answer is "are switched off".

wrongOptions.switch off: The bare infinitive (switch off) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The old stadium ...... (knock down) next year.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is next year, so the correct answer is "will be knocked down".

wrongOptions.knock down: The bare infinitive (knock down) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Active voice puts the wrong focus here — passive needs be + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses be + past participle — the focus is on the action, not the doer.

items.sentence: Her name ...... (leave out) of the competition list.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is her name omitted, so the correct answer is "was left out".

wrongOptions.leave out: The bare infinitive (leave out) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (leave out) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: The students ...... (pick up) after the school trip.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is after the trip, so the correct answer is "were picked up".

wrongOptions.pick up: The bare infinitive (pick up) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Active voice puts the wrong focus here — passive needs be + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses be + past participle — the focus is on the action, not the doer.

exercises.title: F: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct passive form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct passive form.

items.sentence: The sandwiches is prepared every morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.

wrongOptions.is: "is" is the highlighted error — use "are" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "is", not elsewhere — replace it with "are" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: The sandwiches <mistake>is</mistake> prepared every morning.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: The sandwiches are prepared every morning.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The sandwiches are prepared every morning.

items.sentence: My bag were found at the station.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.

wrongOptions.were: "were" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "was".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "were" — the rule here needs "was" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: My bag <mistake>were</mistake> found at the station.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: My bag was found at the station.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: My bag was found at the station.

items.sentence: The invitations will send tomorrow.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.will send: "will send" is the highlighted error — use "will be sent" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "will send", not elsewhere — replace it with "will be sent" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: The invitations <mistake>will send</mistake> tomorrow.
explanationContent.fix: "will send" → "will be sent" (apply the correction shown)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: The invitations will be sent tomorrow.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The invitations will be sent tomorrow.

items.sentence: Is the documents printed every day?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.

wrongOptions.Is: "Is" is the highlighted error — use "Are" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "Is", not elsewhere — replace it with "Are" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: <mistake>Is</mistake> the documents printed every day?
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Are the documents printed every day?
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Are the documents printed every day?

items.sentence: The classroom wasn't decorate for the party.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.decorate: "decorate" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "decorated".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "decorate" — the rule here needs "decorated" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: The classroom wasn't <mistake>decorate</mistake> for the party.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: The classroom wasn't decorated for the party.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The classroom wasn't decorated for the party.

items.sentence: This game play all over the world.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.play: "play" is the highlighted error — use "is played" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "play", not elsewhere — replace it with "is played" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: This game <mistake>play</mistake> all over the world.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: This game is played all over the world.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: This game is played all over the world.

items.sentenceAfter: in the hotel restaurant every morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "is served" — breakfast is served to guests.

wrongOptions.A: "serves" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "is served" is correct.

exercises.title: H: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it means the same as the first.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so it means the same as the first.

items.sentence:
Someone washes these towels every day.
These towels ...... (WASHED) every day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is someone washes, so the correct answer is "are washed".

wrongOptions.washed: The root WASHED cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "are washed".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of WASHED that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: WASHED → are washed: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "are washed" is the word-formation answer from the root WASHED in this context.

items.sentence:
They delivered the furniture this morning.
The furniture ...... (DELIVERED) this morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is they delivered, so the correct answer is "was delivered".

wrongOptions.delivered: The root DELIVERED cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "was delivered".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of DELIVERED that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: DELIVERED → was delivered: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "was delivered" is the word-formation answer from the root DELIVERED in this context.

wrongOptions.published: The root PUBLISHED cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "will be published".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PUBLISHED that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "will be published" is the word-formation answer from the root PUBLISHED in this context.

items.sentence:
Did someone invite Emma to the exhibition?
...... (INVITED) to the exhibition?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is did someone invite?, so the correct answer is "Was Emma invited".

wrongOptions.invited: The root INVITED cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "was emma invited".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of INVITED that fits grammatically, not just the root.

explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "Was Emma invited" is the word-formation answer from the root INVITED in this context.
